challenges.title: URL Component Parser
challenges.description: Write a function `parse_url(url)` that uses `urllib.parse.urlparse` to break a URL into a dictionary with keys: 'protocol' (scheme), 'host' (netloc), 'path', and 'query'. Also write `build_url(protocol, host, path='', query='')` that constructs a URL string from these components.
challenges.starter_code:
from urllib.parse import urlparse, urlencode, urlunsplit

def parse_url(url):
    """Parse a URL into a dict with protocol, host, path, query keys."""
    # Your code here
    pass

def build_url(protocol, host, path='', query=''):
    """Build a URL string from components."""
    # Your code here
    pass
challenges.test_code:
result = parse_url('https://example.com/path/page?name=value')
assert result['protocol'] == 'https'
assert result['host'] == 'example.com'
assert result['path'] == '/path/page'
assert result['query'] == 'name=value'
result2 = parse_url('http://localhost:8080/')
assert result2['protocol'] == 'http'
assert result2['host'] == 'localhost:8080'
assert result2['path'] == '/'
assert result2['query'] == ''
built = build_url('https', 'example.com', '/api', 'key=123')
assert 'https' in built
assert 'example.com' in built
assert '/api' in built
assert 'key=123' in built
simple = build_url('http', 'localhost')
assert 'http' in simple
assert 'localhost' in simple
hints: urlparse returns a named tuple with .scheme, .netloc, .path, .query attributes.
For build_url, you can use an f-string like f'{protocol}://{host}{path}' and append ?query if query is not empty.

challenges.title: Email Message Builder
challenges.description: Write a function `create_email(from_addr, to_addr, subject, body, date=None)` that returns a dict with keys 'From', 'To', 'Subject', 'Date', and 'Body'. If date is None, use 'Thu, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00'. Then write `format_email(email_dict)` that returns a formatted string with headers and body separated by a blank line, like a raw email.
challenges.starter_code:
def create_email(from_addr, to_addr, subject, body, date=None):
    """Create an email message dict with standard headers."""
    # Your code here
    pass

def format_email(email_dict):
    """Format an email dict as a raw email string.
    
    Format:
        From: ...
        To: ...
        Subject: ...
        Date: ...
        
        body text here
    """
    # Your code here
    pass
challenges.test_code:
email = create_email('alice@example.com', 'bob@example.com', 'Hello', 'Hi Bob!')
assert email['From'] == 'alice@example.com'
assert email['To'] == 'bob@example.com'
assert email['Subject'] == 'Hello'
assert email['Body'] == 'Hi Bob!'
assert email['Date'] == 'Thu, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00'
email2 = create_email('a@b.com', 'c@d.com', 'Test', 'Body', date='Mon, 15 Mar 2026 10:30:00')
assert email2['Date'] == 'Mon, 15 Mar 2026 10:30:00'
formatted = format_email(email)
assert 'From: alice@example.com' in formatted
assert 'To: bob@example.com' in formatted
assert 'Subject: Hello' in formatted
assert 'Date: Thu, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00' in formatted
assert 'Hi Bob!' in formatted
lines = formatted.split('\n')
blank_idx = lines.index('')
assert blank_idx > 0
assert 'Hi Bob!' in '\n'.join(lines[blank_idx + 1:])
hints: Use a default value for date: if date is None, set it to the specified default string.
In format_email, join header lines with newlines, add a blank line, then the body.
